rr.topic: How Anthropic trains Claude for judgment/character over rigid rules, and where it still keeps a small set of hard-constraint bright lines — mapped to OSSICRO's rules-vs-concepts validation design

rr.target: OSSICRO anti-slop layer: C:/Users/alto8/OSSICRO/engine/ossicro/register_linter.py, C:/Users/alto8/OSSICRO/engine/registry/banned-constructions.json, and docs/rapid-access-vision-and-plan.md §5 (plus §1 line 17)

critique.issue: The plan frames the deterministic linter as the load-bearing voice mechanism: §1 says 'A register linter makes slop a blocking defect equal to a missing field' and §5 opens with 'Voice quality becomes guaranteeable... a property of architecture.' A 38-regex blocklist cannot guarantee voice — it certifies the absence of 38 known strings, not the presence of professional register.
critique.why: Voice is a quality-shaped property, not a fact-shaped one. LLM mannerisms drift with every model generation ('delve' is already a 2023-24 tell that current models rarely emit), so the list decays silently: it keeps passing while the actual failure surface moves. A clean lint report will become an increasingly hollow assurance — exactly the will-go-stale failure the principal warned about. The architecture that IS a guarantee (fill-only-the-blanks, template-diff invariant, verbatim hashes) guarantees correctness and containment, not voice.

critique.issue: Authority is inverted between §5.1 and §5.2. The plan states 'only the deterministic layer or a human can hard-fail a document — the LLM judge alone fails toward the human gate,' making the regex list the sole automated voice authority and the concept-based reviewer permanently advisory.
critique.why: For a judgment-shaped property, giving the deterministic layer the hard-fail power and the judgment layer none is backwards. The correct asymmetry: the voice agent's findings drive the flag-and-rewrite loop and route to the human gate (a fail-safe direction, so no LLM ever silently blocks or passes anything alone), and it improves for free with every model upgrade. The regex list can never improve without hand edits. Note the plan even sequences the voice agent a full phase later (Phase 3-EA) than the linter (Phase 2.5), so for an entire phase the linter is not just primary but the ONLY voice check.

critique.issue: The density score (weighted findings per 1k words) and passes() threshold in register_linter.py give a false quantitative veneer. A document can score 0.0 while riddled with novel slop, or accumulate density from context-blind low-severity hits ('robust', 'holistic', 'synergy') that the registry's own notes admit are sometimes legitimate.
critique.why: The registry note for 'synergy' says 'keep only for true drug synergy' — but a regex cannot make that distinction; it flags both and delegates the judgment it cannot perform to a density number. Encoding an admittedly context-dependent call as a static severity weight is the rules-bound approach hitting its ceiling. Similarly the 'not-just-but' pattern ([a-z ]{2,40}) will flag legitimate regulatory constructions like 'not only the sponsor but also the investigator must retain records.'

critique.issue: The genuinely rule-worthy content specified in the plan is NOT in the shipped registry, while the fashion-sensitive content is. §5.1 specifies severity-critical exculpatory-consent constructions ('you waive', 'you release', 'the sponsor shall not be liable') cited to 21 CFR 50.20 — none appear in banned-constructions.json. Neither do the promised per-rule confirmed/interpretive classification and citation fields.
critique.why: This is the line-drawing failure in miniature: the one part of the anti-slop spec that is a true bright line (a cited regulatory prohibition — fact-shaped, correct to enforce deterministically forever) went unbuilt, while 30+ mannerism patterns that should be judgment went in as hard rules. In a consent document, missing the 50.20 check is a compliance defect, not a style defect.

critique.issue: Doc/code drift is already visible days after the build: the plan says the registry is 'registry/style-rules.json' driven from check.py; the code ships 'registry/banned-constructions.json' and check.py never calls the linter — nothing in the generation pipeline invokes it (verified by grep; only the test file imports it).
critique.why: The 'deterministic, versioned, defensible' framing depends on the thing actually running on every generation. Right now it is a standalone module with a CLI. Also demonstrates the meta-point: even the rules layer's own spec went stale immediately.

critique.issue: The golden-corpus CI target (§5.4: '≥95% of injected mannerisms flagged') measures recall circularly — the slop set is generated by injecting mannerisms from the same list the linter checks. It can never detect that the list has gone stale against new-model slop.
critique.why: Precision against the golden corpus is a legitimate deterministic regression (protects against flagging real regulatory prose — the controlling failure mode the doc correctly names). But recall against slop-in-general is only measurable by a judge evaluating passages containing mannerisms NOT on the list. Without that, the harness certifies the list agrees with itself.

keep_as_rules: Verbatim-lock SHA-256 checks and the template-diff invariant (§2.2) — byte-level facts; the actual architectural guarantee. Deterministic forever.

Exculpatory consent-language ban ('you waive', 'you release', 'the sponsor shall not be liable', 'you give up the right to'), cited to 21 CFR 50.20 — a regulatory prohibition, i.e., fact-shaped; specified in the plan but currently MISSING from the shipped registry. Build it, with the citation field, as a hard-fail rule.

AI-artifact tells that are categorically never legitimate in a regulatory filing regardless of how writing norms evolve: 'as an AI/language model', 'I hope this helps', 'Certainly!/Great question!' chat-transcript residue. These are transcript-leakage detection, not style opinion — keep as hard-fail regexes (rules ai-self-reference, i-hope-this, certainly-opener).

Mechanically checkable register facts: consent field values in second person, FDA-form worksheets in third person, citation-presence per generated span, statutory clocks (312.310(d) 15-working-day, 56.104(c) 5-day), and the eight non-delegable gates. Structural predicates, not judgment.

Golden-corpus zero-false-positive regression on genuine regulatory prose (FDA model consent language, 1572 commitments, M11 exemplars) — a deterministic CI gate on any change to rules, rubrics, or model version. This half of §5.4 is correct as a rule; only the recall half needs a judge.
move_to_concepts: The ~30 mannerism/promotional/wordiness/hedge patterns (delve, tapestry, testament-to, leverage, seamless, robust, not-just-but, in-conclusion, utilize, plethora, etc.) — fashion-sensitive by nature; the same phrase is slop in one register and precise in another ('synergy' pharmacological vs business, 'robust' statistical vs filler — distinctions a regex admits it cannot make). Demote from blocking rules to (a) a non-blocking smoke-signal tripwire and (b) named failure-class EXAMPLES inside the voice rubric.

The pass/fail authority for voice. register_linter.py's passes() density verdict should never be a release gate. The concept-based voice agent (§5.2) becomes the primary voice mechanism: it judges only filled field values against a small per-category writing-values rubric, returns structured findings {field, span, failure_class, severity, rationale, suggested_rewrite}, drives the flag-and-rewrite loop, and fails toward the human gate — never silently passes, never hard-fails alone. This improves automatically with every model generation; the regex list improves only by hand.

Register appropriateness per document category — consent plain-language/non-minimizing/payment-never-a-benefit, FDA-form factual-transcribable, protocol precise-and-cited, correspondence formal-and-non-speculative. §5.2 already specifies this correctly; elevate it from layer-2-advisory to the primary mechanism and pull it forward from Phase 3-EA into Phase 2.5 so the linter is never the sole voice check for a whole phase.

Overall 'reads as professional regulatory work product' — judged by comparison to reference regulatory documents (the source library), a genuinely conceptual holistic call no enumeration approximates.

Novel-slop recall in the §5.4 harness — add a judge-evaluated lane whose slop set includes fresh mannerisms deliberately NOT drawn from the example list, refreshed as models change, to measure generalization instead of list self-agreement.
rr.recommendation: Keep the linter, demote it, and invert the authority structure. Concretely: (1) Split banned-constructions.json into two tiers: hard-rules.json — verbatim locks, AI-artifact/chat-residue tells, and the 21 CFR 50.20 exculpatory-language patterns (currently unbuilt — build them), each carrying the citation and confirmed/interpretive fields the plan already promises; these hard-fail deterministically forever. And slop-examples.json — the ~30 mannerism/promotional/wordiness patterns, which serve double duty as a millisecond non-blocking tripwire in CI and as few-shot failure-class illustrations embedded in the voice rubric. The examples SEED the concept; they do not DEFINE it. (2) Make the §5.2 voice-validation agent the primary voice mechanism and move it into Phase 2.5 alongside the linter: a small writing-values rubric per document category in registry/voice-rubrics.json (the values: state facts plainly; no promotion, no filler, no metacommentary; register matched to audience and authority), applied with judgment over span-scoped field values, findings failing toward the human gate, model version recorded per finding for the audit trail. This is the Anthropic pattern — character and principles applied with judgment, not a phrase blacklist — and it gets better free with each model. (3) Rewrite the two overclaims: §1's 'a register linter makes slop a blocking defect' and §5's 'guaranteeable' opener. The honest claim: the ARCHITECTURE (fill-only-blanks + verbatim hashes) guarantees containment and correctness; VOICE is assured by layered review — deterministic tripwire, concept-based judge, human gate — with only the tripwire's tier-A rules deterministic. Amend the linter's docstring the same way: 'fast, fallible, explicitly non-authoritative; a clean lint is not a voice claim.' (4) Fix the drift now: wire the linter into check.py (it is currently called by nothing in the pipeline), and reconcile the style-rules.json vs banned-constructions.json filename between doc and code. (5) In the §5.4 harness, keep golden-corpus zero-FP as a hard deterministic gate, but replace the circular '≥95% of injected mannerisms flagged' target with a judge-lane recall measure over slop samples not drawn from the example list. Net effect: determinism stays exactly where facts, citations, statutes, and gates live; everything fashion-sensitive rides the model's improving judgment, with the old rule list preserved as its training examples rather than its ceiling.

rr.target: OSSICRO validation architecture: engine/ossicro/validate.py (rule engine), engine/ossicro/gates.py (non-delegable gates), engine/ossicro/check.py (completeness ledger + cross-document consistency), wiki/05-ossicro-system/generate-check-validate-engine.md, wiki/05-ossicro-system/non-delegable-functions-and-gates.md

critique.issue: R-1571-30DAY (validate.py:67-76) verifies the sponsor's 30-day-wait commitment by substring-matching the rendered prose for the literal tokens '30 days' AND '312.40'. Any legitimate rewording — 'thirty (30) calendar days', '§ 312.40(b)(1)', a restructured commitments section — false-fails; conversely, the two tokens appearing anywhere in unrelated text false-passes.
critique.why: This is exactly the banned-phrase-list pathology the directive warns about: a lexical proxy for a semantic property (does this document express the commitment?). The underlying requirement is a genuine bright line, but the detection is judgment smuggled into a string match, and it goes stale the moment drafting style changes.

critique.issue: R-ICF-50.25 (validate.py:79-92) checks that the informed consent form contains the citation strings '50.25(a)(1)' through '50.25(a)(8)' — it verifies the presence of regulatory markers, not the presence of the eight consent elements. A well-written lay-language ICF for actual patients would not embed CFR citations inline (false fail); a document that pastes the eight marker strings with no substance passes (false pass, and a reward-hack surface for the generate pass).
critique.why: The eight-element list is statutory and correctly bright-line; whether each element is substantively present and adequately expressed in language subjects can understand is a semantic judgment. Checking the proxy token instead of the concept inverts the goal — an ICF optimized to pass this rule gets worse for patients.

critique.issue: R-1572-PHASE (validate.py:55-64) validates phase against a hardcoded closed set {'1','2','3','1/2','2/3','1b/2','2a','2b'} that omits Phase 0 (exploratory IND), Phase 4, '1a', '3b', and prose forms like 'Phase 1b'.
critique.why: Enumeration staleness: the bright line is 'phase must be stated' (21 CFR 312.53(c)(1)(vii)); the whitelist of what counts as a validly stated phase is a normalization judgment frozen into code. The cleaner fix is structural — source phase from the structured study record and render it, making the check a deterministic provenance check rather than a string whitelist.

critique.issue: check_consistency (check.py:28-81) covers only 5 structured fields (CONSISTENCY_KEYS) compared by exact string equality against the study record. Facts asserted in rendered prose — sub-investigator lists, site addresses, dose (which the wiki explicitly claims the consistency engine covers) — escape entirely; and exact match false-positives on formatting variants ('John A. Smith, MD' vs 'John Smith', 'Protocol ABC-123' vs 'ABC-123').
critique.why: Fact identity across documents IS a bright line and the comparison should stay deterministic — but the extraction of fact assertions from prose is a semantic task the current code sidesteps by only checking structured fields. The wiki (generate-check-validate-engine.md line 38) promises broader coverage than check.py delivers.

critique.issue: The wiki's 'adversarial pre-review' — an independent QC agent that red-teams the package for internal contradictions, unsupported spans, stale versions, and missed conditional requirements (generate-check-validate-engine.md line 38) — has no code counterpart anywhere in the engine. The implemented check pass is spine-only.
critique.why: The concept-based layer is designed but unbuilt, so the claim that the check pass 'elevates beyond a checklist' is currently aspirational. This is the single highest-leverage place to add concept-based AI judgment, and it is where model improvement compounds most directly.

critique.issue: Ledger 'green' (check.py:194-205) is annotated 'present; fields filled; validated', but 'validated' means only: fields non-empty plus the 7 shallow rules passed. A document with an inadequate SAE narrative, an incoherent protocol rationale, or a subtly wrong eligibility criterion is green. The system's operating definition of 'complete documentation' ('this ledger with no unexplained reds') therefore overstates what was actually verified.
critique.why: Completeness is deterministic; adequacy is not, and the ledger currently conflates them. The green status needs an explicit adequacy dimension fed by concept-based review, or the claim to reviewers should be narrowed to 'structurally complete'.

critique.issue: Rules are hardcoded Python functions in a static RULES list rather than data (citation + principle text + check strategy). The wiki says every rule 'traces to a specific CFR or ICH subsection' and is surfaced in compliance-mapping, but the principle each rule enforces exists only implicitly in its Python body.
critique.why: This blocks the migration path the directive asks for: a rule whose principle is stated as text can be executed deterministically where the property is factual and handed to a concept reviewer (with the same citation) where the property is semantic. It also makes regulatory-change versioning (the wiki's regulatory-change-log promise) harder.

critique.issue: Cosmetic: the green ledger note (check.py:201-203) computes '%d/%d required fields filled' using len(required_fields) for both numerator and denominator — always n/n by construction.
critique.why: Harmless today (reds already catch missing fields upstream) but it reads as a measured result when it is a tautology; misleading in an audit-facing artifact.

keep_as_rules: All 19 gates in the master gating matrix and the gate mechanics in gates.py (block/route/record/verify, GateViolation fail-closed, named-human + role + attestation requirements, finalize() refusing ungated advancement). These are legally non-delegable under black-letter law — 21 CFR 312.52 means software cannot hold a sponsor obligation, period. This is the exact analogue of Anthropic's hard constraints: a small closed list, code-enforced at the permission layer, no configuration flag that bypasses them, ambiguity resolving toward gating. Determinism here is not a limitation to outgrow; it is the product's legal thesis.

Statutory deadline computation: the 7-day and 15-day IND safety clocks (312.32(c)), the 30-day IND wait (312.40(b)(1)), the 60-day annual report window (312.33). Calendar arithmetic against a regulation is a verifiable fact; an AI 'judgment' about a filing deadline would be strictly worse and indefensible.
Citation presence and traceability: 'a rule without a citation does not ship', and every ledger question carrying its governing CFR/ICH cite. Whether a citation exists and resolves is checkable fact.

Ledger mechanics: the required-documents roster (once fixed for a study), required-field presence, the green/amber/red tri-state, and the invariant that no gated document is green without a recorded sign-off. Presence/absence is fact; the tri-state is the auditable spine.

Cross-document identity comparison on canonical structured fields (PI name, IND number, protocol number/version/title) — after normalization. Two documents asserting different IND numbers is a fact conflict, and the resolution correctly routes to a human question rather than auto-correcting.

The provenance contract: every generated span carries (source → provenance → citation) or is marked inferred. The enforcement mechanic is deterministic even where span classification is model-assisted.

R-SAE-CAUSALITY's never-auto-resolve behavior (validate.py:126-144): deterministically detecting that a serious report lacks a human determination and failing toward the gate. This rule is the template for the whole architecture — the machine computes state and deadlines, the human owns the judgment.

Part 11 audit-trail requirements (model + version + input hash + timestamp + reviewer identity per AI contribution) and the §11.10(a) validation of the deterministic rules as conventional software — this classification is also what keeps the engine in a defensible tier under the FDA AI-credibility draft guidance.
move_to_concepts: ICF eight-element verification (R-ICF-50.25): keep the statutory list of eight elements as the deterministic frame, but assess each element's substantive presence, accuracy, and lay-readability via concept-based AI review with 50.25(a)(n) as the cited principle per element. The current marker-substring check is the clearest case in the codebase of a rule that should be a judgment.

R-1571-30DAY commitment detection: whether prose expresses the 30-day-wait commitment is semantic. Two acceptable fixes, in preference order: (a) restructure generation so the commitment is a deterministic template span verified by provenance (deterministic-by-construction beats both string-matching and AI judgment), or (b) concept-based check citing 312.40(b)(1). Never a substring match.

Narrative adequacy everywhere: is this SAE narrative sufficient for a medical monitor to assess causality; is the protocol rationale coherent; does the IB summary support the expectedness table. No deterministic rule can grade these, the current engine silently skips them (they hide inside green), and they are exactly what improves as the model improves.

The adversarial QC pre-review (already designed in the wiki, unimplemented in code): a structurally independent reviewer agent — no shared working context with the drafting agent — hunting internal contradictions, unsupported spans, stale versions, missed conditional requirements. Inherently concept-based; build it as the check pass's second half.

Risk-proportionate essential-records selection: E6(R3) Appendix C makes which records are essential depend on trial design and materiality — that derivation is judgment. Concept-based AI proposes the required-documents set with reasoning, a human confirms it, and it then FREEZES into the deterministic ledger roster for the study. Judgment at configuration time, determinism at run time.

Fact extraction from rendered prose for the consistency engine: finding where a sub-investigator list, address, or dose is asserted in free text is semantic extraction (concept); once extracted, the equality comparison stays deterministic and mismatches route to a human question. Split the mechanism, don't move the whole thing.

Near-miss equivalence on identity fields: 'John A. Smith, MD' vs 'John Smith' should be flagged by concept-based equivalence assessment as 'probably the same person — confirm', not hard-failed as a conflict or silently exact-match-missed. The AI proposes equivalence; the human disposition is recorded; the canonical record stays the deterministic reference.

Phase recognition (R-1572-PHASE): retire the string whitelist. Preferably make phase a structured enum sourced from the study record and rendered into the form (provenance check, deterministic); where free text must be interpreted, that interpretation is a concept task, not a frozen set literal.

Register/voice/professional-framing review of all outgoing documents: whether a cover letter, IRB response, or 3926 narrative reads as professionally framed for its audience is pure judgment and was never in the rule engine at all — it belongs in the concept lane feeding the foregrounded review UX.
rr.recommendation: Adopt a two-layer architecture with an asymmetric coupling rule, mirroring Anthropic's constitution pattern (few hard constraints, character-and-judgment everywhere else). Layer 1, the deterministic spine: gates, statutory clocks, citation traceability, field/document presence, ledger tri-state, structured-field identity comparison, provenance enforcement, Part 11 audit trail. This layer is validated as conventional software under §11.10(a), is the regression harness, and never grows semantic checks — when a property can be made factual by construction (template spans with provenance, structured enums rendered into forms), prefer that restructuring over either string-matching or AI judgment. Layer 2, the concept reviewer: a structurally independent AI pass where each 'rule' is a cited principle in text form ('21 CFR 50.25(a)(4): the ICF must disclose reasonably foreseeable risks — assess whether this draft does so substantively, in language the subject population can understand') rather than a matcher. Its findings are graded (deficient / adequate / advisory) with quoted evidence and the same resolving-question format the ledger already uses. The coupling rule that makes this safe: concept findings can only ESCALATE — add reds or advisories to the ledger, enrich the human reviewer's critique packet, open a gate earlier — and can never clear: never red-to-green, never discharge a gate, never alter a deadline, never auto-resolve. Green then means 'structurally complete AND no unresolved concept deficiencies', restoring honesty to the completeness claim. Convert validate.py's RULES from Python closures to rules-as-data carrying {citation, principle text, check strategy: deterministic | provenance | concept}, so the three rules currently faking semantics with substrings (R-ICF-50.25, R-1571-30DAY, R-1572-PHASE) migrate to concept or provenance strategies without touching the engine, and future regulatory changes version the principle text rather than reworking matchers. Log every concept-layer contribution per the existing Part 11 attribution scheme (model, version, input hash, reviewer disposition) — this also keeps the concept layer inside the FDA AI-credibility framing already argued in the wiki: low-influence COU because a named human still makes every decision, with the machine critique in front of them. Net effect: the spine never goes stale because it holds only facts, deadlines, and legally non-delegable acts; everything that CAN improve with the model — adequacy, voice, contradiction-hunting, prose-fact extraction, risk-proportionate scoping — sits in the concept layer where a better model automatically means better review, with no rule rewrite required.

synth.validation_philosophy_md:
# OSSICRO Validation Philosophy

**Version 1.0 — 2026-07-09.** This document is dated and versioned because the boundary it draws moves as model capability and regulatory practice move. It is a perpetual work in progress by design, following the precedent of Anthropic's Constitution, which frames itself the same way.

## 1. The default is concept-based judgment

OSSICRO validates generated documents the way Anthropic aligns Claude: a small, named set of hard constraints, and judgment against stated principles everywhere else. Anthropic states the preference directly — "We generally favor cultivating good values and judgment over strict rules and decision procedures" — and gives the reason: a mix of good judgment and a minimal set of well-understood rules generalizes better than rules imposed as unexplained constraints.

The mechanism behind the preference matters more than the preference itself. A deterministic rule encodes a *proxy* for a goal, frozen at the capability level and writing norms of the day it was written. As models improve, a frozen proxy decays three ways at once:

1. **It under-fires.** New failure modes and new phrasings the rule-writer never saw pass clean. A 2024 mannerism list does not catch 2026 mannerisms.
2. **It over-fires.** The model becomes capable of the correct judgment call, but the rule still forces the cruder behavior, producing false positives that erode reviewer trust. Regulatory register legitimately uses "shall", formal passives, and verbatim defined-term repetition that naive style rules punish.
3. **It becomes gameable.** A generation pass optimizing against a fixed surface pattern routes around the pattern while violating the intent. A rule that checks for the string "50.25(a)(4)" trains the generator to paste the string, not to disclose the risks.

A principle stated at the level of intent — "this consent form must disclose reasonably foreseeable risks substantively, in language the subject population can understand" — does not decay. The same principle text produces better application as the reviewing model's judgment improves, because the principle names the target rather than a proxy for it. Capability growth directly improves principle application; it does nothing for a frozen rule. This is why concept-based review is OSSICRO's default: it is the only validation posture that gets better for free.

There is a second, subtler cost of rule-heavy validation that Anthropic documents from its own training experience: narrow mechanical rules do not stay narrow. A system optimized to pass a phrase-scanner learns box-ticking as its operating posture — "did I avoid the forbidden words" instead of "would a payer or regulator reading this find it credible and complete." The rule corrupts the objective, not just the coverage.

## 2. The hard-rule test

The default reverses only when a check passes the test Anthropic's Constitution states for its own hard constraints. A deterministic rule beats judgment when at least one of three conditions holds:

1. **The cost of error is severe or irreversible enough that predictability and evaluability become critical.** A hallucinated citation in a filing is a sanctionable event; a missed statutory deadline forecloses the patient's access.
2. **Judgment is known to be insufficiently robust at the tail.** Calendar arithmetic against a regulation is a case where an AI "judgment" would be strictly worse than a computation, always.
3. **Any flexibility creates an exploitable incentive.** A gate that can be argued around will be argued around; the firmness is itself the protection.

Importance alone does not earn a bright line. Anthropic's sharpest data point here is that honesty — its most load-bearing value — is deliberately *not* a hard constraint, because honesty requires contextual calibration a rigid rule would mangle. OSSICRO applies the same discipline: "this check feels high-stakes" is not an argument for determinism. Only the three conditions are.

## 3. OSSICRO's hard rules (the closed list)

Each entry is here because the target itself is discrete — fact-shaped or legally non-delegable — not because rules are inherently safer.

| Hard rule | Why it qualifies |
|---|---|
| Verbatim-lock SHA-256 checks and the template-diff invariant | Byte identity is a fact. This is the architectural guarantee of containment. |
| Statutory clocks: 7/15-day IND safety reports (21 CFR 312.32(c)), 30-day IND wait (312.40(b)(1)), 60-day annual report window (312.33) | Calendar arithmetic against a regulation. Condition 1 and 2. |
| The non-delegable gates (`gates.py`): named-human sign-off, causality determination, consent, attestation; `finalize()` refuses ungated advancement | Black-letter law — software cannot hold a sponsor obligation under 21 CFR 312.52, and firmness is protective against "just this once" erosion. Conditions 1 and 3. This is the exact analogue of Anthropic's hard-constraint list, and it is the product's legal thesis, not a limitation to outgrow. |
| Citation presence and traceability: a rule without a citation does not ship; every generated span carries provenance or is marked inferred | Whether a citation exists and resolves is checkable fact. |
| Ledger mechanics: required-document roster, required-field presence, green/amber/red tri-state, no gated document green without a recorded sign-off | Presence/absence is fact; the tri-state is the auditable spine. |
| Cross-document identity comparison on canonical structured fields, after normalization | Two documents asserting different IND numbers is a fact conflict; resolution routes to a human. |
| Exculpatory consent constructions ("you waive", "you release", "the sponsor shall not be liable"), cited to 21 CFR 50.20 | A cited regulatory prohibition — fact-shaped, correct to enforce deterministically forever. |
| Chat-transcript residue ("as an AI", "I hope this helps", chatbot openers) | Categorically never legitimate in a regulatory filing regardless of how writing norms evolve. Drafting-process leakage detection, not style opinion. |
| Part 11 audit trail: model, version, input hash, timestamp, reviewer identity per AI contribution | The accountability substrate for everything else, including the concept layer. |

This list is closed and each entry is named with its justification, so reviewers can see at a glance which checks are deliberately rigid and why. Additions require passing the three-condition test in §2, in writing.

## 4. The boundary doctrine

**Fact-shaped properties get rules. Quality-shaped properties get judgment.** A citation exists or it does not; a deadline is met or missed; a hash matches or it does not; a gate has a recorded sign-off or it does not. Whether an SAE narrative is sufficient for a medical monitor, whether a consent form's risk disclosure is substantively adequate in lay language, whether a cover letter reads as professionally framed for its audience — these have no ground truth to string-match against, and the right answer is genuinely contextual.

Three corollaries govern the boundary:

**Never fake semantics with a string match.** When a check needs to know what prose *means*, there are exactly two honest options, in preference order: (a) restructure generation so the property becomes factual by construction — the 30-day-wait commitment as a verbatim template span verified by provenance, phase as a structured enum rendered into the form — because deterministic-by-construction beats both string-matching and AI judgment; or (b) hand the check to the concept reviewer with the citation as its principle. A substring match on "30 days" and "312.40" is judgment smuggled into a lexical proxy, and it inherits all three decay modes of §1.

**Label proxies as proxies.** Any pattern that stands in for a concept states, adjacent to the pattern, what concept it proxies. That is what lets a reviewer — human or model — recognize when the proxy has decayed and retire it, instead of the proxy silently going stale with no signal.

**Rules are data, not code.** Every validation rule carries `{citation, principle text, check strategy: deterministic | provenance | concept}`. A rule whose principle is stated as text can be executed deterministically where the property is factual and handed to the concept reviewer — with the same citation — where it is semantic. Regulatory changes then version the principle text rather than reworking matchers.

## 5. How the concept-based reviewer works

The reviewer is a Claude SDK agent applying Anthropic's own critique-and-revise pattern to OSSICRO's outputs. Constitutional AI works by having a model critique a draft against a sampled natural-language principle and revise in light of the critique; OSSICRO's review pass is the same loop at inference time:

- **Principles, not patterns.** Each concept check is a cited principle in text form — "21 CFR 50.25(a)(4): the ICF must disclose reasonably foreseeable risks; assess whether this draft does so substantively, in language the subject population can understand" — not a matcher. Voice review applies `docs/WRITING-PRINCIPLES.md` per document category.
- **Span-scoped jurisdiction.** The reviewer judges filled field values and generated spans. Verbatim-locked and fixed template prose are outside its jurisdiction by construction.
- **Structured, graded findings.** Each finding is `{field, span, principle cited, grade: deficient | adequate | advisory, quoted evidence, rationale, suggested rewrite}` — the same resolving-question format the ledger already uses.
- **Escalate-only coupling.** Concept findings can add reds or advisories to the ledger, enrich the human reviewer's critique packet, drive the flag-and-rewrite loop, and open a gate earlier. They can never clear: never red-to-green, never discharge a gate, never alter a deadline, never auto-resolve. Hard-fail authority stays with the deterministic layer and humans; the reviewer alone fails toward the human gate. "Green" means structurally complete *and* no unresolved concept deficiencies.
- **Structural independence.** The adversarial QC reviewer shares no working context with the drafting agents — it hunts internal contradictions, unsupported spans, stale versions, and missed conditional requirements as an outsider.
- **Full attribution.** Every concept-layer contribution is logged per the Part 11 scheme: model, version, input hash, finding, human disposition. This keeps the concept layer in a defensible tier under the FDA AI-credibility framing — low-influence context of use, because a named human still makes every decision, with the machine critique in front of them.

The property that justifies the whole design: when the underlying model improves, this reviewer improves with no rule rewrite, no re-labeling campaign, and no registry edit. The principle text stays; the application gets better.

## 6. Accountability for judgment checks

Judgment sacrifices some of what rules provide — up-front predictability, mechanical evaluability. Concept-based checks therefore carry their own accountability, different in kind but not lesser: per-document logged reasoning (auditable after the fact), human spot-check review on a sample, and a hindsight feedback loop — when a document fails downstream (payer rejection, IRB return, regulator flag) despite passing concept review, the failure feeds a rubric revision, recorded in the rubric's version history.

## 7. Revision

The hard-rule list (§3), the writing principles, and the per-category rubrics are versioned, dated artifacts. The example tiers that seed the concept reviewer are *expected* to go stale and be retired; that is their design, not their failure. Proposals to move a check across the rules/concepts boundary cite the three-condition test and are reviewed like any other regulatory-logic change.
synth.writing_principles_md:
# OSSICRO Writing Principles

**Version 1.0 — 2026-07-09.** Applied with judgment by the voice-validation reviewer (plan §5.2) and by human reviewers. This is a rubric of principles, not a checklist of phrases: the operative question for any span is never "does it match a listed pattern" but "would a careful regulatory professional write this, for this reader, in this document." See `docs/VALIDATION-PHILOSOPHY.md` for why the principles govern and the examples merely illustrate.

## The principles

**1. Every sentence serves the reader's decision.**
A regulator, IRB member, or manufacturer reads this document to decide something. Write what that decision needs — the fact, the citation, the commitment — and nothing that exists to perform thoroughness. A sentence that could be deleted without changing what the reader can decide should be deleted.

**2. Evidence over emphasis.**
State facts plainly and let them carry their own weight. Importance claims without content ("plays a critical role"), promotional framing ("promising", "state-of-the-art"), and superlatives are defects everywhere in regulatory prose and severity-critical in consent and counterparty-facing text, where they can minimize risk or overstate benefit. Nothing in a filing is impressive; it is supported or it is not.

**3. Calibrated uncertainty, stated once.**
Where uncertainty is real, state it precisely, once, with its source ("response rates in the phase 1 cohort ranged 20–35%"), not as stacked modals ("may potentially"). False confidence is the same defect in the other direction. The hedge is information or it is noise.

**4. The document does not narrate itself.**
No metacommentary ("it is important to note", "in conclusion"), no framing of what the document is about to do, no summaries of what it just did. A regulatory document ends with the requirement, not a wrap-up.

**5. Register is per-document and per-audience.**
Consent: second person, plain language a subject population can understand, risks stated without minimization, payment never framed as a benefit. FDA forms: third person, factual, transcribable, no narrative or editorializing. Protocol: precise, cited, non-promotional. Correspondence: formal, non-speculative, comparable to a reference regulatory document. The same sentence can be correct in one register and a defect in another.

**6. Write as the signer.**
A licensed physician signs this document as their own professional work product and carries its accountability. The test for any generated span: would that signer, reading closely, adopt this sentence as their own — or wince.

**7. Precision beats variety.**
Defined terms repeat verbatim; elegant variation is a defect here, not a virtue. Formal passives, "shall", and boilerplate repetition are legitimate regulatory register — a reviewer that flags them has misjudged the domain. When in doubt, compare against the source library's reference documents, not against general prose norms.

**8. Nothing reveals the drafting process.**
No chat residue, no AI self-reference, no instruction leakage, no traces of the generation pipeline. (This class alone is enforced deterministically as a hard rule — see `docs/VALIDATION-PHILOSOPHY.md` §3 — because it is fact-shaped and never becomes legitimate.)

## Illustrative examples — non-exhaustive, dated, expected to decay

The following illustrate failure classes as of models circa 2023–2025. They seed the reviewer's judgment; they do not bound it. A span exhibiting none of them can still fail principle review, and several of these phrases are legitimate in the right context — the judgment is the check, not the match.

- *Metacommentary / filler* (principle 4): "it is important to note", "it should be noted that", "in conclusion", "needless to say".
- *Inflation mannerisms* (principle 2): "not just X but Y", "delve into", "a testament to", "rich tapestry", "navigate the complexities", "in today's landscape".
- *Promotional register* (principles 2, 5): "cutting-edge", "seamless", "game-changing", "leverage", "unlock", "empower".
- *Context-dependent terms* (principle 7 cuts both ways): "robust" is filler in marketing copy and precise in a statistics section; "synergy" is business slop except when pharmacological synergy is meant. A pattern-matcher cannot make these calls; the reviewer must.

When a listed example stops appearing in model output, or starts appearing in ordinary professional English, retire it. The registry entry was always a snapshot; these principles are the standard.

## Application

The voice reviewer judges only filled field values and generated spans; verbatim-locked and fixed template prose are outside its jurisdiction by construction. It returns structured findings — `{field, span, principle, severity, rationale, suggested_rewrite}` — that drive the flag-and-rewrite loop and fail toward the human gate. It never hard-fails a document alone and never silently passes one. Findings record the reviewing model's version for the audit trail.
synth.reposition_linter: Reposition as SUBORDINATE HEURISTIC + EXAMPLE-SEED — not deprecated. Concretely: (1) Rename its role from "register linter" to "register tripwire"; its passes()/density verdict is never a release gate. (2) Split banned-constructions.json into two tiers: tier A (registry/hard-rules.json) keeps deterministic hard-fail authority — chat-transcript residue (ai-self-reference, i-hope-this, certainly-opener, feel-free) plus the 21 CFR 50.20 exculpatory-consent constructions, which the plan specified but were never built; each carries citation and confirmed/interpretive fields. Tier B (registry/slop-examples.json) — the ~30 mannerism/promotional/wordiness patterns — becomes a non-blocking smoke signal recorded in the ledger, and its patterns double as the few-shot illustrative examples embedded in the voice reviewer's rubric, explicitly labeled non-exhaustive. The examples SEED the concept; they do not DEFINE it. (3) Voice authority moves to the concept-based reviewer (plan §5.2), pulled forward into Phase 2.5 so the regex list is never the sole voice check; its findings escalate-only toward the human gate. (4) Wire the tripwire into check.py — it is currently called by nothing in the pipeline. One-paragraph reframing now applied to the docstring and engine/README.md: "REGISTER TRIPWIRE: fast, fallible, and non-authoritative for voice. A zero-LLM regex pass that catches known LLM mannerisms in milliseconds. It is deliberately subordinate: it certifies the absence of listed strings, not the presence of professional register, so a clean lint is not a voice claim. Voice authority belongs to the concept-based reviewer applying docs/WRITING-PRINCIPLES.md, whose findings fail toward the human gate and whose judgment improves with every model generation; this list improves only by hand. Two rule classes here are genuine hard rules with deterministic hard-fail authority, because they are fact-shaped and never become legitimate: chat-transcript residue and the 21 CFR 50.20 exculpatory-consent constructions. Everything else is a dated, non-blocking smoke signal whose patterns double as illustrative examples seeding the voice reviewer's rubric. Expect the example tier to decay as models and writing norms change; retire entries freely — the principles, not the patterns, are the standard."

file_changes.summary: The revised validation philosophy: concept-based judgment as the default, the three-condition hard-rule test, the closed hard-rule list with per-entry justification, the fact-shaped/quality-shaped boundary doctrine with three corollaries (never fake semantics with string matches; label proxies; rules-as-data), the escalate-only concept reviewer design, accountability substitutes for judgment checks, and versioning policy.

Eight-principle writing constitution applied with judgment by the voice reviewer and humans; illustrative examples section explicitly labeled non-exhaustive, dated, and expected to decay; application contract (span-scoped jurisdiction, structured findings, fail-toward-human-gate).

Docstring rewritten from 'the deterministic anti-AI-slop substrate' to 'REGISTER TRIPWIRE: fast, fallible, non-authoritative for voice' — names the two genuine tier-A hard-rule classes, declares a clean lint is not a voice claim, points voice authority at WRITING-PRINCIPLES.md, invites retiring stale entries. All 13 engine tests still pass.
Linter and registry table rows reframed to tripwire/two-tier language matching the new docstring.

Seven surgical edits: §1 overclaim ('makes slop a blocking defect equal to a missing field') replaced with layered-review claim; §5 opener 'guaranteeable' overclaim replaced (architecture guarantees containment/correctness; voice is assured by layered review); §5.1 retitled and rewritten as subordinate two-tier tripwire (also fixes the style-rules.json filename drift); §5.2 declared the primary voice mechanism with escalate-only coupling and moved to Phase 2.5; §5.4 circular >=95% recall target replaced with judge-lane recall over non-registry slop (golden-corpus zero-FP stays hard); Phase 2.5 and Phase 3-EA lines updated for the resequencing.

Split banned-constructions.json: tier A = chat-residue rules plus the unbuilt 21 CFR 50.20 exculpatory patterns ('you waive', 'you release', 'the sponsor shall not be liable', 'you give up the right to') with citation and confirmed/interpretive fields, hard-fail; tier B = the ~30 mannerism patterns, non-blocking, embedded as rubric examples. Update test_register_linter.py in the same change.

Per-category rubrics (consent, FDA-form, protocol/M11, correspondence) derived from WRITING-PRINCIPLES.md, with tier-B patterns as labeled non-exhaustive examples; consumed by the §5.2 voice reviewer landing in Phase 2.5.

Wire the tripwire into the check pass (currently only the test file imports it); record tier-B findings as non-blocking ledger annotations and tier-A as reds; accept escalate-only concept-reviewer findings so green means structurally complete AND no unresolved concept deficiencies; fix the tautological n/n field-count note.

Convert RULES to rules-as-data carrying {citation, principle text, strategy: deterministic|provenance|concept}; migrate the three semantic-faking rules — R-1571-30DAY to a provenance-verified template span, R-ICF-50.25 to per-element concept review cited to 50.25(a)(n), R-1572-PHASE to a structured enum rendered from the study record.

Golden-corpus zero-false-positive regression stays a hard deterministic CI gate; add a judge-evaluated recall lane over slop containing mannerisms not drawn from the example registry, refreshed as models change.

key_decisions: Concept-based judgment is the default validation posture; deterministic rules are a closed, named exception admitted only by Anthropic's three-condition test (severe/irreversible error cost, judgment unreliable at the tail, exploitability of flexibility). Importance alone never earns a bright line — the honesty-is-not-a-hard-constraint precedent.

The linter is demoted, not deleted: tier-A hard rules (chat-transcript residue + the unbuilt 21 CFR 50.20 exculpatory patterns) keep deterministic hard-fail forever; the ~30 mannerism patterns become a dated, non-blocking tripwire whose entries double as few-shot examples seeding the voice rubric.

Authority inversion: voice pass/fail moves from the regex density score (never a release gate) to the concept-based reviewer plus the human gate, with an escalate-only coupling rule — concept findings can add reds, drive rewrites, and open gates early, but never clear a red, discharge a gate, or alter a deadline.
The voice reviewer moves from Phase 3-EA to Phase 2.5 so a regex list is never the sole voice check for an entire phase.

Boundary doctrine: fact-shaped properties (hashes, clocks, presence, citations, gates) get rules; quality-shaped properties (adequacy, register, lay-readability, coherence) get judgment. When a check needs semantics, prefer restructuring to fact-by-construction (provenance template spans, structured enums) over both string-matching and AI judgment.
Rewrote the two plan overclaims honestly: the architecture guarantees containment and correctness; voice is assured by layered review, not guaranteed by a linter.

Replaced the circular >=95% injected-mannerism recall target with a judge-lane recall measure over slop not drawn from the example registry; golden-corpus zero-false-positive stays a hard deterministic gate.
All principle documents are versioned, dated, and marked provisional, mirroring the Constitution; the example tier is designed to decay and be retired.

Executed the documentation/repositioning layer in this pass (2 new docs, 3 files edited, tests green); the five engine-code migrations are enumerated as planned changes because they touch the rule engine and test suite and warrant their own reviewed change.
